title: Where Have All the Families Gone? Aging Empty Nesters and Housing Lock-In
gist: Economist Colin Williams uses new neighborhood-scale gridded Census data merged with CoreLogic parcel records to show U.S. neighborhoods converging in age structure, and argues tax policies (Prop 13-style assessment caps, federal step-up basis, senior exemptions) lock aging owners into family-sized homes in high-appreciation metros like LA while fast-building Sun Belt metros like Houston recycle homes to new families.

insights.claim: Houston is green (child-dense) nearly to its core while Los Angeles is mostly orange/tan with children confined to pockets, despite both being sprawling Sun Belt-ish metros of similar size — measured via a Youth-to-Woman Ratio from gridded Census data merged with CoreLogic records.
insights.speaker: Colin Williams
insights.so_what: Quantifies a structural metro divergence in family geography that shapes long-run housing demand, school enrollment, and regional growth.
insights.quote: one is visibly a place that raises children and the other, increasingly, is not
insights.locator: Houston is green nearly to its core

insights.claim: Between 2000 and 2020 the cross-sectional 'hump' linking ~25-year-old housing vintages to high child density nearly vanished — the peak collapsed by about 70% and the gap between most and least family-heavy vintages roughly halved.
insights.speaker: Colin Williams
insights.so_what: National convergence means once child-rich suburbs are reverting to average, eroding the demographic base that supported family-oriented retail and services.
insights.quote: the peak collapsed by about 70 percent
insights.locator: By 2020, that hump had all but vanished

insights.claim: Houston's oldest neighborhoods recovered to within a few percent of their 2000 child density as new families moved in, while Los Angeles's oldest neighborhoods kept declining, losing roughly a quarter of their children over 20 years.
insights.speaker: Colin Williams
insights.so_what: The same vintage of homes turns over in Texas but stays locked in California — a direct read on which metros can regenerate family housing supply.
insights.quote: The same houses that turn over to young families in Texas stay locked in place in California.
insights.locator: But at the old end of the housing stock

insights.claim: In high-appreciation metros the tax code rewards aging owners for not selling via three policies: property-tax assessment caps (e.g., California's Prop 13), the federal step-up basis that erases lifetime capital gains at death, and senior property-tax exemptions with residency requirements that reset on a move.
insights.speaker: Colin Williams
insights.so_what: Identifies specific, addressable tax levers behind frozen housing turnover — actionable for housing-policy and homebuilder theses.
insights.quote: the tax code rewards aging homeowners for not selling
insights.locator: For an old neighborhood to welcome young families

insights.claim: California softened Prop 13 in 2020 by letting older owners carry their low assessment to a new home, but only one inside the state, so a senior following children to Texas or Arizona still forfeits the benefit.
insights.speaker: Colin Williams
insights.so_what: The in-state-only portability deepens California's lock-in relative to fast-growing Sun Belt destinations, reinforcing interstate migration patterns.

insights.claim: Nearly 18% of California property transfers now happen through inheritance — double the 8.8% national share — as owners hold appreciated homes until death, per a 2026 WSJ report by Friedman and Dagher.
insights.speaker: Colin Williams
insights.so_what: A hard national-data fingerprint of lock-in; signals how thin the resale supply is in capped-assessment markets.
insights.quote: nearly 18% of property transfers now happen through inheritance—double the 8.8% national share
insights.locator: In a 2026 story for the Wall Street Journal

insights.claim: Williams warns Georgia's 2024 HB 581, which caps annual assessed-value growth at inflation, recreates the Prop 13 trap by opening a widening wedge between assessed and market value that penalizes movers (though localities may opt out).
insights.speaker: Colin Williams
insights.so_what: Flags policy contagion risk — new assessment caps could spread Sun Belt lock-in and choke off the turnover advantage those metros currently enjoy.
insights.quote: should resist new assessment caps like Georgia’s recent HB 581 that recreate the same trap
insights.locator: Make tax benefits portable, not place-bound

insights.claim: Family-sized housing in safe, high-opportunity neighborhoods is a binding constraint on family formation; a 2026 Couillard paper finds adding large units generates more than twice the births per dollar of adding small units.
insights.speaker: Colin Williams
insights.so_what: Directly ties housing-unit mix to fertility — a pronatalist case for large-unit construction and a metric (births per dollar) for evaluating housing policy.
insights.quote: adding large units generates more than twice the births per dollar of adding small ones
insights.locator: Family-sized housing in safe, high-opportunity neighborhoods

insights.claim: Williams is explicit that the cross-metro correlation between lock-in policies and demographic rebound is suggestive, not causal, because assessment caps, restrictive zoning, scarce construction, and rapid price growth all travel together.
insights.speaker: Colin Williams
insights.so_what: Honest caveat to weight before treating tax reform as a guaranteed fix; the confound is the broader supply-restriction bundle.
insights.quote: The cross-metro correlation between lock-in policies and demographic rebound is suggestive, not causal
insights.locator: Finally, I want to be candid
